actions.thought: T4s is a very weak hand, even in a 3-handed game. Opening with marginal to weak hands like this often leads to difficult post-flop spots and unnecessary chip loss. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

Jack-Trey offsuit is a very weak hand. Even in a three-handed game, completing the small blind with such low equity and being out of position for the rest of the hand is generally negative expected value. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack at the start of the tournament.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. While the button is the best position to steal the blinds, J6o is a very weak hand and generally falls outside a standard opening range even in a 3-handed game. Since we are only at the second hand of the tournament with deep stacks (100BB), there is no reason to take an unnecessary risk with a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind with 5d-Tc, which is a weak hand, but I am facing a heads-up situation against the big blind after the button folded. With the pot odds offered, completing the small blind to see a flop is a reasonable, low-risk approach to playing this marginal hand from out of position.

I hold AK offsuit in the Big Blind and the Small Blind has limped in. This is a premium starting hand that strongly dominates the typical limping range of the Small Blind. Raising is the optimal play here to extract value, build the pot in position, and punish the passive action.

Facing a raise from the big blind with a weak offsuit hand (5d-Tc) while playing out of position is a losing proposition. The pot odds do not justify chasing with such a marginal holding, and folding is the most disciplined action to preserve my stack for a more favorable spot.

The hand Jd 4s is a weak, uncoordinated hand that does not warrant an investment of 100 chips from the button. At this early stage of the tournament, preserving the stack is more important than playing marginal hands.

With 32o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I hold the absolute worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite the temptation to attempt a steal, doing so from out of position with a hand that has virtually no playability or equity post-flop is a losing long-term strategy. I will fold and save my chips for better opportunities.

In a 3-handed game, the Button should open a wide range. K5s is a solid candidate to raise first in, as it has good playability and steal value. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to put pressure on the blinds while maintaining position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ten of Hearts and Four of Hearts. Pro has raised to 250 from the Button. While T4s has some flush potential, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. Given the tournament structure and the early stage, it is better to fold this hand and preserve my stack rather than calling and playing a weak hand out of position for the rest of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The Button opened to 250. Given the favorable pot odds and the likelihood of a wide range from the Button, defending with Ace-high is a standard play. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity while keeping the pot size manageable, rather than bloating it with a 3-bet against a potentially strong range.

I am out of position as the Big Blind and hold middle pair (seven) with an Ace kicker. Since I am the first to act on the flop, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (the Button) is the standard and most strategic play. This allows me to gauge their action and control the pot size while avoiding leading into a range that likely connects well with the King-high board.

We flopped top pair but have a weak kicker on a draw-heavy board (two clubs). Checking behind is reasonable for pot control, but a small continuation bet of around 1/3 to 1/2 pot gets value from worse hands like flush draws, straight draws, and lower pocket pairs, while protecting our equity.

I am facing a bet of 200 into a pot of 550, providing me with approximately 3.75:1 pot odds. My middle pair (sevens) with an Ace kicker remains a viable holding on this board. Since the bet is relatively small, folding would be overly cautious, and raising would only serve to isolate me against the stronger part of the opponent's range. Calling is the most balanced approach, as it allows me to realize my equity and keep the opponent's betting range wide while controlling the pot size.

I am out of position as the Big Blind and hold a marginal hand (middle pair with sevens). Since the turn card (3d) is a relative blank and I am acting first, checking is the most strategic play to control the pot size and evaluate the opponent's strength. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, and checking gives me the opportunity to reach showdown affordably.

The 3d on the turn is mostly a blank. We still have top pair, but our weak kicker makes it risky to build a large pot. Checking behind allows us to exercise pot control, keep Flashlight's bluffs and weaker marginal hands in their range, and potentially induce a bluff on the river or comfortably value bet/call depending on the river action. It prevents us from value-owning ourselves against better Kings.

The river is a 9s, which does not improve my hand. My pair of sevens is weak and serves primarily as a bluff catcher. Since the opponent checked behind on the turn, betting now is unlikely to extract value from worse hands and might fold out bluffs I want to keep in. Checking is the most prudent path to reach showdown and control the pot size.

With top pair and a weak kicker on a board where all the draws missed, Flashlight's river check likely indicates a marginal made hand (like a 7, 9, or a middle pocket pair) or a busted draw. While we could try to extract thin value from a lower pair, a bet runs the risk of only being called by better Kings (K6+) or inducing a check-raise bluff. Our hand has solid showdown value but is too weak to bet comfortably for value. Checking behind secures our equity, ensuring we get to showdown without value-owning ourselves.

I am on the button with 8-6 offsuit. While I have positional advantage, 8-6o is a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. I have played conservatively so far, and there is no reason to start splashing around with marginal hands this early in the tournament. Folding is the most prudent play here.

I am in the small blind with a weak, unsuited hand (4-9 offsuit). Investing more chips into a weak starting hand out of position against the big blind is strategically unsound. Folding is the standard, disciplined choice.

Queen-five offsuit is a weak hand with no playability. There is no incentive to enter the pot from the button with such a poor holding. Folding is the disciplined, standard play here to preserve my stack.

With Q5o in the Small Blind against the Big Blind after the Button folds, my hand is too weak to play for a raise or a limp. The positional disadvantage post-flop combined with a very weak offsuit queen makes folding the most fundamentally sound decision. I will wait for better spots to commit chips.
3d 6d is a weak suited three-gapper. Even on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand is too weak to play profitably. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 4s. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight) to act after me. While 84s has some potential due to its suitedness, it is generally too weak to play profitably out of position, especially since Flashlight has shown a tendency to call raises in previous hands (Hand 4). Folding here is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9-4 offsuit in a three-handed game. This hand is significantly below the opening range for a button raise, even in a short-handed format. Given its poor playability and low equity, folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

QTo is a reasonably strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. By opening the pot with a raise to 300, I take the initiative and exert pressure on the Big Blind, which is a standard aggressive strategy in this position.

Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, 98o is too weak to defend with, despite being in position post-flop. The odds are 2:1, but the hand lacks suitedness and high card strength. Folding is the disciplined play.

My hole cards are 2d and 4c, which are extremely weak and hold very low equity against any range. In a three-handed game, even from the button, playing such a hand is a losing long-term strategy. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation to enter the pot.
Folded to me in the Small Blind. A2o is a reasonable hand to attempt a steal against the Big Blind. Raising to 300 puts pressure on Flash to fold out his marginal holdings.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Hearts and Five of Clubs. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I am getting 2:1 on a call, Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand to defend even against a potentially wide Small Blind range. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.
I am holding 9-2 offsuit on the button, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. There is no reason to play this hand, so I will fold.

With the button folding, I am in a blind-vs-blind situation. 89o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the Small Blind to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the blinds immediately. Although I will be out of position post-flop, the hand has decent playability.

The hand 6-3 suited is objectively weak, especially out of position. Even though I am receiving reasonable pot odds, the long-term expectation of defending this hand against a small blind open is negative. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve stack depth for more playable holdings.

I am on the button with T3s. In a three-handed game, the opening range from the button is typically wide, but T3s is at the very bottom of that range. Given that the Big Blind ('Pro') has shown some aggression in previous hands and the Small Blind ('Flashlight') has been relatively tight, I could attempt a steal. however, T3s lacks significant post-flop value if called. I will opt for a disciplined fold here to wait for a stronger hand or a better spot to apply pressure, as we are still early in the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight suited, and the Button folded. I am heads-up against the Big Blind. Raising from the Small Blind is a standard strategy to exert pressure and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to accomplish this.
I have 23 offsuit in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Small Blind. This is one of the worst starting hands in poker and an easy fold.

I am on the button with AJo, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Opening the pot from the button is the standard strategic move here to steal the blinds or take initiative in the hand. A raise to 250 is a standard opening size in this structure.
I am in the Small Blind facing a raise to 250 from the Button. My hand is 2s 4d, which is completely disconnected and one of the worst hands in Texas Hold'em. This is a very easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7c 4c. Flashlight raised to 250 from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 550. This gives me pot odds of approximately 27%. 74s is a marginal hand, but against a wide Button opening range, it typically has enough equity to justify a defense, especially given the suited connectivity. I will call and play cautiously post-flop, looking for a strong draw or two-pair.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw (4-5-6-7, needing a 3 or 8) on a board of Ah-6d-5h. Although the Ace is likely to have hit the Button's opening range, I have significant equity with my draw. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to them and see how they respond. I intend to call a reasonably sized bet to try and realize my equity or potentially semi-bluff later if the board texture allows.

I have top pair with a good kicker on a relatively dry board. As the pre-flop aggressor, checking behind would show weakness and allow my opponent to realize equity for free. A continuation bet of 275, representing half the pot, is the optimal play to extract value from weaker aces, middle pairs, and to protect my hand against potential straight draws like 7-8.

I have an open-ended straight draw (4-5-6-7) on a board of Ah-6d-5h. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 275 into a 550 pot. This gives me pot odds of 25% (275 to win a total pot of 1100). My 8 outs to a straight give me roughly 17% equity on the turn and about 31% by the river. Given the potential for implied odds if I hit my straight against an Ace-x range, calling is a mathematically sound decision to attempt to realize my equity.

On the turn, the 3s has completed my straight (3-4-5-6-7). This is currently the best possible hand (the nuts), as there is no 8 on the board to allow for a higher straight. Flashlight has been the aggressor throughout the hand, raising pre-flop and betting on the flop. By checking, I am trapping to induce a bet from Flashlight's range, which likely includes top-pair (Aces), sets, or heart-flush draws. This strategy maximizes value by allowing Flashlight to continue their aggression before I commit more chips to the pot.

I hold top pair with the top kicker, which is a commanding hand on this relatively dry, uncoordinated board. My opponent check-called on the flop, indicating they likely have a weaker Ace, a middle pair, or a draw. I want to continue extracting value while charging potential draws (like 4x or 78s) to see the river. Checking would allow for a free card, which is unnecessary here. A bet of 550 is roughly half the pot, providing a solid size to build the pot for a river value bet while maintaining a manageable risk.

I have successfully turned the current nuts with a 7-high straight (3-4-5-6-7) on a board of Ah-6d-5h-3s. Flashlight has continued their aggression with a bet of 550 into a pot of 1100. While I initially checked to trap, the board contains a heart flush draw, making it risky to give a free card. A raise here serves multiple purposes: it builds the pot for a potential river shove, extracts value from top-pair hands (like Ax) that Flashlight is likely to hold, and charges any heart draws. Given the stack sizes and the pot size, a raise to 1650 (3x the bet) is a strong value play that maximizes my potential return on this hand.

The opponent's turn check-raise on this 5-6-A-3 board is a strong indicator of a made hand like a set or two-pair, though the straight draw 2-4 is also possible. Given the pot odds of 3:1, calling with top pair, top kicker is mathematically correct. Folding top pair would be too weak given the stack depth and the board texture, but raising is risky as it likely commits my stack to a situation where I am dominated. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate.

The board is 5h-6d-Ah-3s-4s, making a 5-card straight on the board (3-4-5-6) with an Ace. I hold the 7c, which gives me a 7-high straight (3-4-5-6-7). This hand is extremely strong, though it is vulnerable to an 8-high straight if the opponent holds 7-8. Given the action (Flashlight raised pre-flop, bet flop, and called my turn raise), they likely hold a strong hand like top pair (Ax), a set, or a straight themselves. Since I act first on the river, I will place a significant value bet of 3,000. This amount is large enough to extract maximum value from Ace-high hands or sets that might feel committed to the pot, while also being a hand that chops with any other 7. If the opponent has 7-8, it is a cooler, but my 7-high straight is too strong to play passively here.

The board has completed a straight (2-3-4-5-6), and the opponent's aggressive line of a turn check-raise followed by a river lead on such a coordinated board strongly indicates a made straight or a disguised two-pair. My top pair, top kicker is now a weak bluff-catcher that is significantly behind the opponent's value range. Given the high probability of the opponent holding a 2, 5-6, or set, and the lack of a better hand to beat, folding is the disciplined professional play to preserve the remainder of my stack.
KJo is a solid starting hand 3-handed, especially on the button. Raising to 2.5x (250) to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a strong broadway hand is the standard and optimal play.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-4 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5bb. This is a very weak hand to defend with, especially out of position. Even with 2:1 pot odds, the playability of 8-4o is extremely poor, and I would likely be check-folding most flops or putting myself in a difficult spot. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I hold pocket Kings, which is a premium hand. Pro opened from the button, and I am in the big blind. I should definitely not just call; I want to build the pot and extract value. 3-betting to 850 is a strong, standard play here to take control of the hand and put pressure on the opener.

Flashlight has made a sizable 3-bet from the big blind (from 250 to 850). While KJo is a good hand to open from the button, it performs poorly against a 3-betting range, as it is often dominated by hands like AK, AQ, KQ, AJ, and big pocket pairs. Calling here leaves us in a tough spot post-flop with a high risk of reverse implied odds. Folding is the safest and most profitable long-term decision.

Holding Ace-Queen offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a premium starting hand. My goal is to extract value and gain control of the pot. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the blinds and antes immediately or play a pot in position. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard sizing for this stack depth.

Ac7c is a strong suited ace, suitable for defending against a button raise. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the hand has significant equity and post-flop playability. Calling allows me to see the flop with a reasonable price, keeping the pot manageable, whereas a 3-bet could lead to an uncomfortable 4-bet scenario that forces a fold.

K2s is too weak to play multiway from the big blind against a 3x open from the button and a call from the small blind. Reverse implied odds when I hit a king are high, and realizing equity out of position will be very difficult. Folding is the safest and most standard choice to preserve the stack.

The board texture is rainbow and disconnected (Qh-9s-3d), and my hand is essentially Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. Since I am out of position as the Small Blind, checking is the standard play to gauge the opponent's strength and control the pot size.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK). The board of Q-9-3 is relatively dry, although there are some straight draws like J-T. Flashlight checked to me, which is a standard move for the pre-flop caller. I should bet here to extract value from hands like weaker Queens, 9x, or pocket pairs that might call. A bet of about half the pot (350) is a standard and effective sizing to build the pot and protect my hand.

The opponent has made a continuation bet on a relatively dry board (Qh-9s-3d). With Ace-high, I have reasonable showdown value and backdoor equity. Given the pot odds of 3:1 (calling 350 to win a total pot of 1400), it is mathematically sound to call and re-evaluate on the turn. Folding would be too conservative, and raising as a bluff is unnecessary at this stage.

I am the Small Blind and it is my action on the turn. The board is Qh-9s-3d-Kd, which is relatively dry but now includes a King. With Ace-high and no pair, my hand lacks showdown value against a range that includes strong Kings or Queens. Betting into the opponent is ineffective, and I prefer to check to see if the opponent checks behind for a free river card or to re-evaluate their strength if they choose to bet. Given the current board texture and my position out of the button, check-folding or check-calling are my only real options depending on the size of a potential future bet.

The King of Diamonds is an overcard to my pair of Queens. While I still have a strong hand with second pair and an Ace kicker, the turn card could have easily improved Flashlight's range (specifically hands like KJs or KTs that might have floated the flop). By checking back, I am exercising pot control and ensuring I reach the river with a manageable pot. This also allows me to see what Flashlight does on the river and potentially catch a bluff or value bet if they check again.

The board has paired on the river (9s-9d), and I hold Ace-high (Ac7c). The opponent (Flash) checked back the turn, which often indicates they have a marginal holding like a medium pair or are checking to control the pot size. Given that I have only Ace-high and cannot beat any pair, betting as a bluff is unlikely to succeed against a range that includes many pairs or pocket pairs. Checking is the most prudent decision to reach showdown as cheaply as possible, as my hand has no showdown value against anything better than total air, and I am not representing a strong hand that would justify a bluff.

On this board texture (Q-9-3-K-9 with three diamonds), my pair of Queens with an Ace kicker has significant showdown value but is vulnerable. When Flashlight checks for the third time, it's possible they are holding a King, a Nine, or a completed diamond flush and are looking to check-call or trap. Alternatively, they might have a weaker Queen or a pocket pair like Tens or Jacks. By checking back, I ensure I reach showdown without risking a check-raise on a dangerous board where very few worse hands would call a value bet. Checking back is the safest and most standard line for pot control with second pair.

I am dealt pocket Kings on the button, which is an extremely strong hand. I need to open the betting to build the pot, isolate, and extract value from the blinds. A standard raise to 300 (3 big blinds) is appropriate here to put pressure on my opponents and maintain control of the hand.

Pocket Tens is a strong hand that performs best heads-up. Facing a button raise, 3-betting from the Small Blind builds the pot for value, protects our hand by pushing out the Big Blind, and puts pressure on the original raiser's wider opening range. Sizing to around 1000 (roughly 3.3x) is a standard out-of-position 3-bet size.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs7s. The Button (Flashlight) opened to 300, and the Small Blind (Pro) 3-bet to 1000. While Q7s has some playability, calling a 10x 3-bet out of position against two active players is a losing proposition. My hand is dominated by the Small Blind's 3-betting range, and I do not have the right odds or position to continue. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold pocket Kings, which is a powerhouse hand. Pro has 3-bet me, signaling strength, but I need to assert dominance. A 4-bet to 2600 allows me to build a massive pot, maximize value against range, and define the strength of Pro's hand early. It is too early to just call; I need to get chips in the pot while I have the advantage.

Facing a large 4-bet from the Button, our pocket Tens are put in a very tough spot. Flashlight's 4-bet to 2600 represents a strong range, heavily weighted toward higher pocket pairs (JJ-AA) and strong broadways (AK, AQs). Against that range, we are either flipping or severely crushed. Calling just to set-mine is mathematically unprofitable here, as the remaining effective stack (around 4475) does not offer the implied odds needed to call a 1600-chip bet. Additionally, 5-bet shoving is too risky because Flashlight has already committed a significant portion of their stack and is unlikely to fold their strong hands. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play.

On the button in a 3-handed game, 94 suited is far too weak to open-raise or call. Even with a wide button stealing range, this hand falls well outside of acceptable starting requirements. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 2s, which is a very weak hand. The Button has folded, so it's just me and the Big Blind. While I have the chip lead, 62o has almost no playability post-flop and is not a good candidate for a steal attempt against an opponent like Flashlight, who has shown recent aggression. Folding is the most disciplined play here to conserve chips for better opportunities.

I am on the button with KQo, which is a very strong hand in 3-handed play. My goal is to open for a standard raise to build the pot and take control of the hand with positional advantage. A 3x raise to 300 is standard and consistent with the table's recent betting patterns.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. Flash has raised to 300 from the Button. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the pot odds offered (needing to call 250 to win a pot that will be 600+) make this a reasonable call to see a flop, especially since QTo has decent playability. A 3-bet here could get me into trouble if Flash decides to 4-bet, and folding might be slightly too tight given the stack depth and the relative strength of my hand. I will call and see how the flop develops.

We are getting excellent pot odds (200 to call for a pot of 700) to close the pre-flop action with a premium suited connector, 65s. Suited connectors play very well in multiway pots because of their ability to make strong, disguised straights and flushes, offering great implied odds. Although we will be out of position against the Button post-flop, the deep stacks (around 90 BBs effective) and our hand's high playability make calling the remaining 200 chips a profitable decision.

The board of 3d-Kc-Th is King-high, which heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I hold a pair of Tens, which is a marginal made hand that is effectively a bluff catcher. Being out of position against two other players, checking is the most strategic move to control the pot size and see how my opponents react to this texture. Betting into two players here would likely only be called by better hands and force out bluffs, which does not provide value.

With 6s 5s on a 3d Kc Th flop, we completely missed the board, having no pair and no draw. The pre-flop raiser (Flash) is still left to act behind us. Since we have virtually no equity and it's a multiway pot, checking with the intention to fold to any bet is the only viable option.

I have top pair with a strong queen kicker on a board that is relatively favorable for my pre-flop raising range. Both opponents have checked to me, which often indicates weakness or a desire to control the pot. I will bet for value and to protect my hand against potential straight draws like QJ or J9, while also getting called by weaker kings or middle pairs.

The bet from Flash is 500 into a 900 chip pot, offering me roughly 2.8:1 pot odds. My pair of Tens is a reasonable bluff catcher in this spot, especially on a board that is not extremely coordinated. While a King is certainly in the aggressor's range, folding would be overly tight given the price I am being laid and the potential for the opponent to be continuation betting with a wider range of hands, such as air or smaller pairs. I will call to see how the turn develops, keeping the pot manageable and maintaining my equity in the hand.

With 6-high and absolutely no piece of the board or draws on a 3d Kc Th flop, and facing a bet from the pre-flop raiser and a call from the Small Blind, I have virtually zero equity. The only viable play is to fold and preserve my stack.

The turn card, an eight of spades, is relatively safe and does not significantly change the board texture for my opponent's range. As I am out of position with a pair of tens, my hand functions primarily as a bluff catcher. Checking is the most strategic play here, as it allows me to control the pot size and see if the opponent continues their aggression with a bet, while avoiding overcommitting with a hand that loses to any King or better pocket pair.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a relatively dry board. Flashlight checked to me again after calling my flop bet, which typically suggests a marginal made hand like a weaker King (KJ, K9) or a draw (QJ, J9). Since the 8s is mostly a blank, I will continue betting for value and to protect my hand against potential straight draws. A bet of 1000 into a 1900 pot (roughly 53% pot) is a standard sizing to extract value while keeping their range wide enough to call.

On the turn, the board is 3d-Kc-Th-8s and my pair of Tens is effectively a bluff catcher. The opponent, Flash, has shown consistent aggression throughout the hand, and given the board texture, a King is a very strong component of their betting range. While the pot odds are somewhat tempting, my equity against a standard button-opening range that hits this flop is quite low. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic move at this stage, as it preserves my remaining stack of 7425 chips for a more favorable situation, avoiding a potentially costly showdown against a likely better hand.

I am in position on the button with pocket Jacks, which is a very strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I should look to build a pot and potentially isolate the blinds. A standard raise to 300 chips (3x the big blind) is appropriate to take control of the hand.

King-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand, and facing a 3x open raise from the Button, it is an easy fold from the Small Blind. Calling puts me out of position with a marginal holding, and 3-betting as a bluff with this specific hand is not optimal.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit. Flashlight (the Button) has raised to 3x the big blind. Given the pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 450) and the fact that King-high hands have reasonable equity against a standard button opening range, this is a standard defend. I have a significant stack advantage and can afford to see a flop to see if I connect with a King or a pair of eights. 3-betting is an option but calling allows me to play post-flop without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind and the pre-flop caller. The flop is Ah-Jd-9d, which is very heavy with high cards and a diamond draw. This board significantly favors the button's opening range. I have King-high with no diamonds and no piece of the board. The standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and likely fold to any significant bet, as I have very little equity to continue.

I have flopped a set of Jacks on an A-J-9 board. This is an incredibly strong hand that is currently beating top pair (Ace) or two-pair combinations that my opponent might hold. With the board showing two diamonds, there is a flush draw, so it is important to bet for value and to protect against potential draws. I will bet 350 into the 650 pot to build the pot and get value from hands like A-X or secondary pairs.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit on a flop of Ah-Jd-9d. This board is extremely favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Aces, Jacks, and diamond draws. I have no pair, no draw, and no backdoor equity. Facing a bet of 350 into a 650 pot, I do not have the required equity to continue. King-high is unlikely to be the best hand now or by the river, and I have very few favorable turn cards. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.
I am on the Button with an extremely weak starting hand (3-4 offsuit). Attempting to steal the blinds with this hand is not profitable long-term, so a fold is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Three of Clubs (T3c) after the Button has folded. While it's a blind-vs-blind situation and the cost to call is only 50 chips, T3c is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. My opponent in the Big Blind (Flashlight) has demonstrated aggressive play in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined choice to avoid getting involved in a marginal pot out of position with a sub-par hand.

I have pocket Kings on the button, which is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My objective is to build a significant pot and gain value from my opponents. I will make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300 chips). This allows me to take control of the hand and extract value from weaker hands that might call from the blinds.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong hand that plays well as a 3-bet against a button open. By 3-betting, I take the initiative and force the opponent to react, rather than playing passively out of position against an aggressive opener. This size allows me to define my range and potentially take down the pot immediately or set up a favorable continuation bet.

KQo is a decent hand in a vacuum, but facing an open from the Button and a strong 3-bet from the Small Blind, it performs poorly against their typical continuing ranges (AK, AQ, JJ+). Calling 900 chips out of position with a highly dominated hand is unprofitable, and a 4-bet bluff is overly risky at this stage. Folding is the most standard and safest decision.

I have pocket Kings, which is a monster hand. After my initial raise from the button was met with a 3-bet to 1,000 from the small blind, the best play is to 4-bet for value. This builds a larger pot and prices in weaker hands while setting up a potential all-in. I will raise to 2,500 to put pressure on Flashlight's stack and capitalize on my strong holding.

Facing a 4-bet of 2500 after my initial 3-bet to 1000, I am now in a difficult spot out of position. While my Ace-Jack suited has decent equity against some of Flash's aggressive range, their 4-bet demonstrates significant strength or a commitment to the pot. Given the low stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) if I call, I would essentially be committing myself to the hand on most flops. Without a premium hand like QQ+ or AK, calling and playing a large pot out of position is high-variance and often suboptimal. I choose to fold to preserve my remaining stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with King-Five suited, which is a playable hand in a three-handed game. I want to utilize my position to steal the blinds and take control of the pot. A standard raise to 250 should be sufficient to test the waters and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a C-bet on favorable textures.

Ace-Five offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a button raise, calling from the small blind is not advised as we will be out of position post-flop and the Big Blind could still squeeze. 3-betting is an option, but we prefer suited Aces for that. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Four offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 2.5bb (250 chips), and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 chips into a pot that currently has 400 chips. The pot odds are 2.67:1, meaning I need roughly 27% equity to justify a call. Ace-Four offsuit is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a Button open. Although I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the strength of the Ace and the favorable price make this a clear call rather than a fold or a 3-bet raise.

On a flop of Js 3s 6h, I have Ace-high with a backdoor straight draw. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, Flashlight. Standard play in this situation is to check to the aggressor to see how they react to the board. My Ace-high has some showdown value and could potentially improve on future streets, but it's not strong enough to lead out with a bet here.

I have a King-high flush draw on a rainbow board with a Jack. Since the opponent checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play to exert pressure, fold out weak hands that might have a small pair or high cards, and build the pot for when I hit my flush. Betting roughly 55% of the pot (300 into 550) is a solid sizing to achieve these goals.

On a board of Js 3s 6h, I am holding Ace-high (Ac 4d) with no significant draws. The Button (Flashlight) has made a continuation bet of 300 into a 550 pot. While my pot odds are roughly 26%, Ace-high is very vulnerable here. I am out of position, and the Jack-high board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Without a spade in my hand or any straight potential, I have very little equity to realize on future streets. Calling would likely lead to me folding on the turn to a second barrel, and raising as a bluff is unnecessary given my chip lead and the lack of blockers in my hand. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button 3-handed with T9o. While some offsuit connectors can be opened in short-handed play, T9o is fairly marginal, and with aggressive opponents in the blinds (Flash and Flashlight have shown a willingness to 3-bet), folding is the most solid and standard play to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 2d. The Button has folded. While it's only 50 more to call, 8-2 suited is a very weak hand to play out of position against the Big Blind, who has shown a willingness to be aggressive in previous hands. Given my current chip lead, there is no need to play such a marginal hand and risk being put in a difficult spot post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with King-Nine offsuit. This is a standard opening hand. My stack is significantly larger than both opponents, allowing me to apply pressure. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate to try and take down the blinds or play a pot in position against the Small Blind or Big Blind.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Nine offsuit against an aggressive button raiser. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, and calling a raise of 300 to invest another 250 chips into a tough spot OOP is negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

My hand is 3h 2h, which is the worst suited connector. Facing a 3bb raise from the button, I am out of position and my hand lacks the equity and playability to profitably call or 3-bet. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

I am dealt 3-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With no equity to speak of and needing to maintain stack discipline in a 150-hand tournament, playing this hand would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct decision here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind and the action is folded to me. My hand is Ten-Three offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand and generally unprofitable to play even from the small blind. The best action is to fold.

K7o is a relatively weak offsuit hand to open with, even from the button. To avoid unnecessarily bleeding chips and getting into tricky post-flop spots without a strong range advantage, folding is the standard and safest play.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of sixes. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind (Flashlight). Pocket sixes is a very strong hand in a blind versus blind situation. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately or build a pot with a hand that has good showdown value.

The opponent in the small blind has raised to 300. Holding King-eight suited, I have a reasonable hand to defend my big blind. Given the pot odds and position, calling 200 more to see a flop is the most calculated decision to preserve my stack while maintaining pressure.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with pocket sixes and am now heads-up against the Big Blind. The flop (Qh-7s-8h) is somewhat coordinated with both straight and flush possibilities. While my pair of sixes is relatively weak on this board, I will make a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to maintain the initiative and potentially take the pot down immediately if the opponent missed. If I am called, I will likely proceed with caution on the turn, as this board hits a lot of the Big Blind's defending range.

I have middle pair (8s) on a rainbow board with a queen and a flush draw present. The opponent's bet is 300 into a pot of 600, offering 3:1 pot odds. While my king-kicker provides little value against a queen, my pair is strong enough to justify a call to see a turn card. I will keep the pot manageable and re-evaluate on the next street.

The turn card, the Ace of Hearts, is a very coordinated and dangerous card. It completes a possible flush and adds another overcard to my pocket sixes. Given that Flashlight called my flop bet, their range likely includes pieces of this board or draws that may have improved. Continuing to bet into this board with only a pair of sixes is risky, as I am unlikely to fold out better hands and will almost certainly be called by hands that beat me. I will check to control the pot and evaluate the opponent's action.

I have bottom/middle pair (8s) on a board that is now Ace-high with a potential flush draw. Since Flash checked the turn, he may be pot-controlling or trying to induce a bluff. My hand has some showdown value but is likely behind any Ace or better-made hand. Betting into him would likely only be called by stronger hands or folds by worse, offering little value. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and take a free look at the river.

The river is a 3s, which is a blank and doesn't change the board texture. My pocket sixes have very little showdown value given the board (Qh-7s-8h-Ah-3s). I checked the turn after being the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop. Flashlight also checked the turn, suggesting they might have a marginal hand or a draw. While I could attempt a bluff, my hand has a small amount of showdown value against a total air-ball bluff or a missed straight draw that doesn't bet. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or fold if they bet, as my hand is unlikely to be the best if they commit more chips.

The river is a 3s, which does not improve my hand. My pair of eights is vulnerable to any Ace or Queen, and even smaller pairs if the opponent held them. Since the opponent checked the turn and river, they likely have a marginal hand or a missed draw. Betting would likely force a fold from hands I beat or induce a call from hands that beat me. Checking behind is the most conservative play, allowing me to take the showdown with my current pair of eights without risking further chips.

I am on the button with 7s 6d in a three-handed game. While the button is the best position to steal blinds, 76o is at the very bottom of an opening range. Given that Flashlight (SB) has shown significant aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hand #15 and #20), I expect a higher frequency of 3-bets or calls from the small blind. I'll choose to fold this marginal hand to maintain a tighter image and avoid playing a bloated pot out of position or against a range that likely dominates mine.
I am in the small blind with K7 offsuit, which is a weak hand to play out of position against the big blind. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

My starting hand, Ten of Diamonds and Trey of Diamonds, is quite weak. Even though I am on the button, this hand does not have sufficient equity to merit an open raise or a call against the blinds. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand given the tournament context.

J2s is a very weak starting hand, even suited. While we are heads-up against the Big Blind, playing out of position with such marginal holdings is unprofitable in the long run. The standard and most sensible play is to fold pre-flop and save our chips.

I am on the Button with A8s, a strong hand that plays well in position. It has decent high-card value, blockers to strong aces, and excellent nut-flush potential. I will open-raise to a standard 2.5x to take the initiative and try to win the blinds or play a pot in position.

I have Pocket Tens, which is a very strong premium hand in a 3-handed game. The player on the Button (Pro) has opened for 250. Given I am in the Small Blind and will be out of position for the rest of the hand, I should 3-bet to take control of the pot and define ranges. A 3-bet to 850 is a standard sizing that applies pressure to the Button's wide opening range and discourages the Big Blind from entering with marginal hands.

K-3 offsuit is a very weak hand with almost no potential to improve against a standard button raise and a subsequent 3-bet from the small blind. The pot odds do not justify a call in this spot. Folding is the only mathematically sound decision.

Flash has 3-bet my Button open from the Small Blind. While Ad8d is suited and contains an Ace blocker, it is often dominated by a standard SB 3-betting range, which likely includes higher Aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) and strong pocket pairs. Playing a bloated pot post-flop with a potentially dominated hand can lead to significant chip loss, even with positional advantage. To protect my stack and wait for a more profitable spot, folding is the disciplined choice here.

I am on the button with 9h 6s. While opening from the button is generally a good strategy to pick up the blinds, 96o is at the bottom of the range and does not play well post-flop. Both opponents have shown willingness to defend or re-raise. Given my chip lead and the poor quality of my hand, folding is the most prudent play to avoid unnecessary variance.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 2h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. Despite the small investment of 50 chips already in the pot, playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is strategically unwise and would likely lead to losing more chips in future streets. Preserving the stack for better equity spots is the priority.

With King-Ten offsuit on the button in a three-handed game, this is a strong enough hand to open the betting and attempt to take down the blinds or play in position. Given the aggressive nature of the table, a raise to 300 is a standard sizing to potentially steal the pot or set up a favorable post-flop scenario.

Holding 8-2 offsuit in the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button. This is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em and completely unplayable. The standard and optimal play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Flashlight raised from the button to 300. While button opening ranges are wide in 3-handed play, 8-3 offsuit has very poor equity and playability post-flop. There is no reason to defend such a weak hand here, especially with a healthy stack and many hands remaining in the tournament.

I am on the Button with Queen-Six offsuit, which is a very weak and disconnected hand. Even though I have position, opening this hand is not profitable over the long term, especially against capable opponents in the blinds. The best play is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

With Ace-Ten of spades in the small blind and the button folding, I have a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and extract value with a hand that has significant equity and post-flop playability.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Five offsuit, a weak, disconnected hand with poor playability. While I am getting a reasonable price to call, Q5o is easily dominated by the range of an opponent raising from the Small Blind, and playing this hand out of position post-flop will likely lead to difficult decisions and chip loss. Given the tournament stage and my current stack, folding is the most disciplined and strategic move.

On the button in a 3-handed game, I have a wide opening range. Queen-Five suited is a standard open to apply pressure on the blinds, especially as the chip leader. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is consistent with my previous play and allows me to take control of the pot or win the blinds immediately.

I have pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With an aggressive opponent on the button who has been active in recent hands, a 3-bet is the optimal strategic move. It allows me to build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially isolate the button, all while discouraging callers behind me. Raising to 1000 is a standard, strong sizing that sets up for a potentially large pot.
I am dealt 3d 2s, which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Furthermore, I am facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind. This is a trivial fold.

I opened with Qc 5c from the button to apply pressure, but Flashlight responded with a 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind. Queen-Five suited is too weak to call a 3-bet out of position or to justify a 4-bet bluff in this spot, especially since Flashlight has shown aggression in previous hands. I will fold and maintain my chip lead.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game, holding Ace-Eight offsuit. In a 3-max format, the button is the first to act pre-flop. Holding an Ace with an eight kicker is a hand I want to open with to attempt to steal the blinds or take initiative in the hand, given my positional advantage. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that balances pot commitment with gathering information.

Q9o is a weak offsuit hand that performs poorly out of position. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, it is best to fold from the Small Blind, avoiding putting chips in with a marginal holding while still having the Big Blind left to act behind me.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. Flashlight opened from the Button to 300. While the odds to call are roughly 2.25 to 1, this hand is extremely weak and plays very poorly out of position. Given the stack depths and the tendency of opponents to be aggressive, I will fold and wait for a better starting hand.
J5 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor equity and bad playability post-flop. Even on the button, it is a clear fold to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight) to contend with. While King-high has some showdown value in a blind-vs-blind battle, King-Trey is a very weak hand to play out of position against an opponent who has shown recent aggression. Given my stack lead, I prefer to wait for better spots rather than bloating a pot with a marginal hand out of position.

I am on the button with 8s3s. While suited, this is a very weak hand. I have a large stack of 13,825, but there is no need to play trash hands from the button when the blinds are still 50/100 and I have plenty of hands remaining in the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak, unsuited hand (Jh 4c). With no action from the Button (Flash folded), I am facing the Big Blind (Pro). Given the poor quality of my hole cards and the fact that I would be playing out of position post-flop, the most disciplined and professional decision is to fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

Jack-Eight offsuit is a weak, marginal holding that does not merit continuing, especially when facing a raise or even just playing from the button. It lacks the connectivity and high-card strength to defend or profitably open the pot given the tournament state. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

With a small pocket pair (33) in the small blind and the action folded to me, I have a great opportunity to raise and try to steal the big blind. If Flash calls, I still have equity and the chance to flop a well-disguised set. Raising helps define his range and takes the initiative going into the flop.

I have been dealt Pocket Queens in the Big Blind. Flashlight folded, and Pro raised to 300 from the Small Blind. This is a very strong premium hand. I should 3-bet for value and to take control of the hand. Raising to 900 is a standard sizing against an SB open to build the pot and potentially end the hand right here or set up a post-flop advantage.

Facing a 3-bet from the big blind after my steal attempt, my pocket 3s lose a lot of their appeal. Being out of position, the implied odds for set-mining are not quite favorable enough (we typically want 15-20 times the call amount in the effective stack, and we only have about 12 times). Playing a small pair out of position in a 3-bet pot is extremely difficult if we don't flop a set. The most disciplined play is to let this go and wait for a better spot.

I am dealt 2h 3c on the Button, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With two disconnected, extremely low, unsuited cards, there is zero value in playing this hand. The mathematically and strategically sound choice is an immediate fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 86s, which is a strong speculative hand for a blind-vs-blind battle. Since the button (Pro) folded, I only have Flashlight in the Big Blind to beat. Raising here allows me to pick up the blinds immediately or take the initiative into the flop with a hand that has good straight and flush potential. Given the stack sizes, a standard 3x raise is appropriate.

Flash is opening frequently from the small blind, suggesting a wide range. QTs is a strong enough hand to defend against a 3x open sizing, especially given the favorable pot odds of 2.5:1. Calling allows me to see a flop with a decent hand and leverage my positional advantage as the Big Blind.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board has an Ace, which fits my perceived range well. However, the board is monotone (all hearts), and I do not have a heart in my hand, which makes this texture dangerous. I have a gutshot straight draw to the seven. I will put out a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to represent the Ace and try to take the pot down now. If Flashlight calls or raises, I will likely proceed with caution or fold to further aggression unless the turn improves my hand significantly.

I have flopped a Queen-high flush draw on a very wet board (4h-Ah-5h). With a bet of 300 into a 600 pot, I am getting 3:1 pot odds, making this a mathematically sound call given my equity. I also hold the Queen of Hearts, which is a useful blocker. Raising on this texture is risky given the likelihood of an Ace in the opponent's range, so I will stick to calling to see the turn and re-evaluate.

The turn card (Jc) doesn't change much on this monotone board. My flop continuation bet was called, which suggests Flashlight likely has a piece of the board (an Ace or a heart). With only a gutshot straight draw and no hearts in my hand, I have very little equity. I will check here to see if Flashlight checks behind, potentially giving me a free river card, or to fold if they put out a substantial bet.

The turn checks to me, and I hold the Queen of hearts, giving me a strong flush draw on this wet board. Given Flash's aggressive pre-flop and flop betting, checking here is a prudent way to take a free card and realize my equity. If I hit my heart on the river, I will likely have the winning hand. If I miss, I can safely check-fold unless the river improves my hand significantly. Checking allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity without committing more chips on this street.

The river completes a four-flush on the board (4h-Ah-5h-Jc-7h). While I have made a straight (4-5-6-7-8), it is extremely vulnerable as any single heart in Flashlight's hand beats me. Flashlight called my flop bet on a monotone board, which strongly suggests they have either an Ace or a heart. After we both checked the turn, the 7h on the river is a terrible card for my hand. I will check and hope for a cheap showdown, but I am prepared to fold to any significant bet because it is highly probable the opponent has a heart.

I have rivered a Queen-high flush on a board with four hearts (Ah, 7h, 5h, 4h) by holding the Qh. Since my opponent checked, they likely do not have the King of Hearts for the nut flush, or they are playing cautiously. Betting for value is the standard play here to get called by an Ace or a smaller heart, given the pot size of 1200.

On the river, the 7h completes a four-flush on the board (4h-Ah-5h-Jc-7h). I have a straight (4-5-6-7-8), but any single heart in Flashlight's hand makes a flush and beats me. Flashlight called my flop bet, which is consistent with holding an Ace or a heart draw. When I checked the turn and the river, Flashlight bet 700 into a 1200 pot. On a four-flush board, it is highly likely that Flashlight either has a heart or is representing one. Given that I have no heart in my hand to act as a blocker and the straight is significantly devalued on this texture, calling is risky. Most non-flush hands like a simple Ace would likely check back for showdown value rather than betting. I will fold.

I am on the button with King-Five suited. In a three-handed game, this is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or play in position. My stack size is healthy, and a standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit, which is a weak starting hand. Flash has raised from the Button, and calling would force me to play out of position for the entire hand with a marginal holding. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack, folding is the disciplined and correct decision here.

Facing a 3bb raise from the Button, King-Four suited in the Big Blind is generally too weak to defend profitably out of position. The reverse implied odds of hitting a weak top pair and the difficulty of realizing equity OOP make folding the most standard and solid choice.

I am on the button with Ace-Four suited in a 3-handed game. This is a strong position to open the betting and attempt to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. My hand has good equity, and playing aggressively pre-flop is the standard strategy for this stack size and position.

I am dealt Ten-Deuce offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight raised from the Button to 300. Considering my terrible starting hand and the positional disadvantage, folding is the only profitable move to conserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Q2s. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the button. While I have the chip lead and the pot odds are roughly 3.25:1, Q2s is a very weak hand to defend out of position. Flashlight has been active, and I'd prefer a stronger hand to contest the pot or a better candidate for a 3-bet bluff. I will fold and maintain my stack.

K8s is a strong enough hand to open from the Button in a 3-handed game. It has good playability post-flop and blocker value. I will make a standard 2.5x open raise to 250 chips to attempt to steal the blinds or play in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened for 250. While I have the chip lead, Q6o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff with this hand is not ideal given the stack sizes and current table dynamics. The most disciplined play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 3c, which is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Even though I am getting 2.6-to-1 pot odds, playing such a weak hand out of position against an aggressive button raiser is poor strategy. Saving chips for better opportunities is the better play here.

I am on the button with Qh5h, which is a reasonable hand to open for a steal in a 3-handed game. I have the largest stack and can apply pressure to the blinds. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and should be enough to take down the pot or allow me to play a pot in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 7d, which is a marginal suited connector. Calling out of position against an aggressive opponent (Flash) who has already opened, and with another player (Pro) yet to act in the Big Blind, is risky and potentially costly. The pot odds do not offer sufficient value to justify a call, and 3-betting is not appropriate for this hand strength. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

We are in the Big Blind with pocket fours facing a standard 3x open from the Button. The effective stack is over 75 big blinds, which gives us excellent implied odds to call and set-mine. A 3-bet could turn our hand into a bluff and force us to fold to a 4-bet, so calling is the most profitable and standard approach.

On a paired Q-8-8 board, our pocket fours are vulnerable and we don't have a strong enough hand to lead out for value or protection. Checking to the preflop raiser is the standard line, allowing us to evaluate their action before making a decision.

I have top pair with a Queen on a paired board (8s 8d). Pro checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot to get value from pocket pairs lower than Queens, spade draws, and some Ace-high hands that might call one street. This also helps protect my hand against various overcards or draws.

Facing a continuation bet on a paired board with a Queen, our pocket fours are heavily counterfeited and very vulnerable. We are out of position against the pre-flop raiser, and almost any turn card or further aggression will be impossible to call. With only two outs to improve to a full house, folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

Holding King-six suited on the button is a marginal hand. While being in position is advantageous, the hand itself lacks sufficient strength to justify an open-raise or a call against aggressive opponents in this three-handed game. Conserving my stack and waiting for a better opportunity is the most disciplined approach.
Q3o is a very weak hand, even in a blind vs blind scenario. Being out of position against the Big Blind makes it highly unprofitable to play. Folding is the standard move to preserve my stack.

J7o is a weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. Even on the button, it is generally not profitable to open or play this hand unless the blinds are extremely tight. Folding is the optimal play here to preserve chips for better starting hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 4d. After the Button folds, the action is on me. 84o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against Flashlight in the Big Blind. While it only costs 50 more to call, there is little incentive to play such a marginal hand when I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack suited, which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. Standard opening size here is between 2.5x to 3x the big blind. Raising allows me to take control of the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop, or play a strong hand in position if called. My stack is healthy, and while Flashlight and Pro have shown some aggression in previous hands, this hand is well within my value raising range.

I am in the Small Blind with K5 offsuit, a weak hand with poor playability, especially when out of position against a button raise. Given the current stack depth and the risk of playing a weak hand against an aggressive opponent, folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision.

A5 suited is a strong hand that plays very well post-flop. Facing a button raise from an active player, I can either 3-bet as a semi-bluff or call. Calling keeps the pot size manageable and allows me to realize my equity with potential for nut flushes and wheel straights.

On a highly connected and wet board like Ks Ac Qc, my top pair with a weak kicker is marginal, as the pre-flop raiser's range connects very well with this texture. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see how Flash reacts before making any significant commitments. I also hold a backdoor spade flush draw, but I want to keep the pot small.

I have top pair with a top kicker (TPTK) on a very wet and coordinated board. While the board texture (K-A-Q) connects well with many hands in the Big Blind's calling range, such as KT, QT, or JT, I still have the best hand frequently against weaker Aces or pairs. I will bet 350, which is slightly over half the pot, to extract value from those weaker made hands and to charge potential draws, while also maintaining pot control in case I am facing a stronger range.

On this extremely wet and connected board, my top pair with a weak kicker is a marginal holding. The board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which contains many better Aces, two pairs, and strong draws. However, facing a standard half-pot continuation bet from the Button, I cannot fold top pair immediately, as Flash will likely c-bet a wide range including weaker draws, underpairs, or total air. I also have a backdoor spade flush draw to add a little equity. I will call to evaluate the turn, keeping the pot manageable and planning to proceed cautiously against further aggression.

The turn brings a Ten, putting a 4-card broadway straight on the board. Any Jack makes a straight. My top pair with a 5 kicker is now very weak, and I am beat by a huge portion of my opponent's range, including straights, two pairs, and sets. Checking is the only sensible move to control the pot. If Flash bets, I will likely have to fold given the scary board texture.

I have completed a Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-T) on the turn. This is an extremely strong hand, although the board is highly coordinated and several full house combinations (AA, KK, QQ, TT, AK, AQ, AT, KQ, KT, QT) are theoretically possible.
